content.text: Log Access Controls and Audit Logging

Claude applications generate logs that touch sensitive territory: request metadata, error details, cost attribution data, and — if PII scrubbing is imperfect — fragments of user context. Controlling who can read those logs and recording every access is not optional compliance theater; it is how you detect insider threats, pass SOC 2 audits, and prove to regulators that access to production logs is narrow and monitored.

This document covers the full control surface: RBAC design for log consumers, immutable audit log stores for privileged operations, break-glass procedures for incidents, and the automated tooling that turns log-access policy into enforceable code.

attrs.alt: Log access controls and audit logging architecture

content.text: Why Log Access Is a Security Problem

Application logs feel low-risk compared with databases, but production logs for Claude applications are often the richest available record of what users typed and what the system did. Even after PII scrubbing:
are correlatable with user sessions in other systems.
often echo fragments of the input that triggered them.
reveal which users are heavy consumers — competitive intelligence.
fingerprints the stack to external observers.
record admin actions that themselves reveal business logic.
The threat model is not just external attackers — it includes over-permissioned developers, contractors with broad access, and automated processes that write to shared Slack channels.
Grant read access to logs at the narrowest scope sufficient for the job. Audit every log query. Treat access to audit logs as itself a privileged operation.
Most log platforms (Loki, CloudWatch, Datadog, Elastic) support some form of label or field-based access control. The following role set covers a typical Claude platform team:
App logs for services they own (filtered by service label)
Audit logs, other teams' services, infra logs
All app logs + all audit logs
Unredacted PII (should never exist in logs)
Cost and usage metrics only (Prometheus / Grafana)
N/A — all access logged with PR reference

Grafana Loki enforces access via tenant isolation combined with the multi-tenancy header and Grafana's data-source permissions. A simpler approach that works without enterprise Loki is to use label-based stream selectors enforced at the proxy layer.

# loki.yaml — enable multi-tenancy
auth_enabled: true

# Each tenant maps to a log stream tag
# Producers set X-Scope-OrgID header based on log_type label
# Vector config adds the header automatically:
#   [sinks.loki_app]
#   tenant_id = "app-logs"
#   [sinks.loki_audit]
#   tenant_id = "audit-logs"

# grafana provisioning: datasources.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Loki-App
    type: loki
    url: http://loki:3100
    jsonData:
      httpHeaderName1: X-Scope-OrgID
    secureJsonData:
      httpHeaderValue1: app-logs          # Only app-log tenant visible to ops-reader

  - name: Loki-Audit
    type: loki
    url: http://loki:3100
    jsonData:
      httpHeaderName1: X-Scope-OrgID
    secureJsonData:
      httpHeaderValue1: audit-logs        # Only exposed to security team data source

# grafana rbac: assign data sources to teams
# REST API call (also works via Grafana Terraform provider)
POST /api/access-control/datasources/<uid>/permissions
{
  "permission": "Query",
  "teamId": 12,           # team ID for "security"
  "datasource": "Loki-Audit"
}
Audit Log Store: Immutable and Separate

Audit logs — records of privileged operations, configuration changes, and access control events — must not share infrastructure with application logs. An attacker (or insider) who compromises the app log store must not be able to alter audit evidence.
Properties of a Compliant Audit Log Store
Once written, records cannot be modified or deleted by any API call. AWS S3 Object Lock, GCS Object Hold, Azure Immutable Blob Storage all satisfy this.
The service account that writes audit logs cannot read them (write-only). The service account that reads audit logs for compliance cannot delete or overwrite them.
Each record includes a hash chain or Merkle-tree anchor so tampering is detectable even if an attacker somehow bypasses the storage lock.
SOC 2, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS all require multi-year audit log retention. Set lifecycle policies defensively at 10 years and let compliance teams reduce if permitted.
Audit log failures must not silently drop — they must be routed to a high-priority queue that pages security, not to the same Slack channel as app-log parse errors.
What to Write to the Audit Log
The audit log captures actions that change state or access sensitive resources. For Claude applications:
actor, principal, old_roles, new_roles, ip, timestamp
actor, query_expr, log_store, time_range, rows_returned, duration_ms, timestamp
Every log access in controlled stores
actor, pr_url, justification, ttl_hours, ip, timestamp
actor, rule_id, old_pattern, new_pattern, change_type, timestamp
actor, flag_name, old_value, new_value, environment, timestamp

import json
import time
import hashlib
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional

# Dedicated audit logger — separate handler, separate file/stream
_audit_log = logging.getLogger("audit")
_audit_log.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# In production: handler points to a write-only stream or firehose
# that feeds the immutable audit store — never the same handler as app logs.

@dataclass
class AuditEvent:
    event_type: str
    actor: str
    resource: str
    action: str
    outcome: str           # "allowed" | "denied" | "error"
    ip_address: str
    timestamp_ns: int
    details: dict
    prev_hash: str = ""    # simple hash chain

    def to_log_dict(self) -> dict:
        d = asdict(self)
        chain_input = f"{self.prev_hash}|{self.timestamp_ns}|{self.event_type}|{self.actor}"
        d["entry_hash"] = hashlib.sha256(chain_input.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        return d


_last_hash = ""

def emit_audit(
    event_type: str,
    actor: str,
    resource: str,
    action: str,
    outcome: str,
    ip_address: str,
    details: Optional[dict] = None,
) -> None:
    global _last_hash
    event = AuditEvent(
        event_type=event_type,
        actor=actor,
        resource=resource,
        action=action,
        outcome=outcome,
        ip_address=ip_address,
        timestamp_ns=time.time_ns(),
        details=details or {},
        prev_hash=_last_hash,
    )
    record = event.to_log_dict()
    _last_hash = record["entry_hash"]
    _audit_log.info(json.dumps(record))


# Usage: API key rotation
emit_audit(
    event_type="api_key_rotated",
    actor="admin@example.com",
    resource="claude-prod-key-007",
    action="rotate",
    outcome="allowed",
    ip_address="10.0.1.44",
    details={"key_scope": "claude:messages", "previous_key_id": "key-007-v3"},
)

# Usage: log query executed
emit_audit(
    event_type="log_query_executed",
    actor="sre-alice",
    resource="loki:audit-logs",
    action="query",
    outcome="allowed",
    ip_address="10.0.1.12",
    details={
        "query_expr": '{log_type="audit"} |= "api_key_rotated"',
        "time_range_hours": 24,
        "rows_returned": 3,
        "duration_ms": 45,
    },
)
The most frequently overlooked audit requirement is recording who queried the logs, not just who wrote them. Every log platform provides query history APIs — use them.
Grafana / Loki Query Audit via Middleware

# FastAPI middleware: intercept Loki query proxy and emit audit event
from fastapi import Request, Response
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
import httpx

class LokiQueryAuditMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next) -> Response:
        # Only audit query endpoints
        if not request.url.path.startswith("/loki/api/v1/query"):
            return await call_next(request)

        actor = request.headers.get("X-User", "unknown")
        ip = request.client.host if request.client else "unknown"
        body = await request.body()

        response = await call_next(request)

        # Parse rows returned from response (approximate from content-length)
        emit_audit(
            event_type="log_query_executed",
            actor=actor,
            resource=f"loki:{request.headers.get('X-Scope-OrgID', 'unknown')}",
            action="query",
            outcome="allowed" if response.status_code < 400 else "denied",
            ip_address=ip,
            details={
                "path": request.url.path,
                "query_params": dict(request.query_params),
                "status_code": response.status_code,
            },
        )
        return response

P0 incidents occasionally require an engineer to access logs beyond their normal permission set — for example, an on-call SRE needing audit logs to investigate a potential credential leak. Break-glass is not an exception to policy; it is a documented, audited policy path.
against the access-control config repo. The PR description includes the incident ticket number and a time-scoped justification.
A second engineer approves the PR (4-eyes principle). Automated checks verify the justification field is non-empty and the requested TTL ≤ 24 hours.
CI merges the PR and immediately calls the IAM API to grant the role with a TTL. It emits an audit event with the PR URL.
During incident, all log queries made under the break-glass role are captured by the query audit middleware (see above).
At TTL expiry, the role is auto-revoked. An automated scanner runs within 1 hour to confirm revocation. If it detects the role still exists, it pages security.
the security team reviews the audit trail of queries made during break-glass. Findings go into the incident retrospective.

import time
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

def grant_break_glass(
    actor: str,
    incident_ticket: str,
    pr_url: str,
    ttl_hours: int,
    ip: str,
) -> str:
    if ttl_hours > 24:
        raise ValueError(f"Break-glass TTL cannot exceed 24h, got {ttl_hours}h")

    expiry = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(hours=ttl_hours)
    expiry_iso = expiry.isoformat()

    # Grant via kubectl RBAC (example for k8s-native audit logs)
    manifest = f"""
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: break-glass-{actor.replace('@', '-')}-{incident_ticket}
  namespace: prod-logs
  annotations:
    expires-at: "{expiry_iso}"
    incident: "{incident_ticket}"
    pr: "{pr_url}"
    actor: "{actor}"
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: log-reader-all
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: {actor}
"""
    subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "apply", "-f", "-"],
        input=manifest.encode(),
        check=True,
    )

    emit_audit(
        event_type="break_glass_granted",
        actor=actor,
        resource="k8s:prod-logs:log-reader-all",
        action="grant",
        outcome="allowed",
        ip_address=ip,
        details={
            "incident_ticket": incident_ticket,
            "pr_url": pr_url,
            "ttl_hours": ttl_hours,
            "expires_at": expiry_iso,
        },
    )
    return expiry_iso
Prometheus Metrics for Log Access Observability
Audit logs record every access, but an alert based on audit logs alone would be slow — you need to detect anomalies in real time. Export access metrics to Prometheus.

from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram

log_queries_total = Counter(
    "log_queries_total",
    "Total log queries executed",
    ["actor_role", "log_store", "outcome"],
)

log_query_duration_seconds = Histogram(
    "log_query_duration_seconds",
    "Log query execution time",
    ["log_store"],
    buckets=[0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 30],
)

break_glass_activations_total = Counter(
    "break_glass_activations_total",
    "Break-glass access grants",
    ["actor_role"],
)

audit_write_failures_total = Counter(
    "audit_write_failures_total",
    "Failed writes to audit log store — must stay at 0",
    ["store"],
)

# alerting rules for log access anomalies
groups:
  - name: log-access
    rules:
      - alert: AuditLogWriteFailure
        expr: increase(audit_write_failures_total[5m]) > 0
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: security
        annotations:
          summary: "Audit log write failed — compliance gap"
          description: "{{ $value }} audit events dropped in last 5m for store={{ $labels.store }}"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/audit-log-write-failure"

      - alert: BreakGlassActivated
        expr: increase(break_glass_activations_total[1h]) > 0
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: security
        annotations:
          summary: "Break-glass access activated"
          description: "{{ $value }} break-glass activations in last hour"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/break-glass-review"

      - alert: LogQueryRateSpike
        expr: |
          rate(log_queries_total{log_store="audit-logs"}[15m]) > 2
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: security
        annotations:
          summary: "Unusually high audit log query rate"
          description: "Audit log query rate >2 req/s — potential bulk export"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/audit-log-query-spike"
CI Enforcement: Access Policy as Code
Log access policy should live in version-controlled YAML, and CI should refuse to merge changes that widen access without a corresponding review trail.

# config/log-access-policy.yaml
roleBindings:
  - role: ops-reader
    canRead:
      - app-logs/team-a
      - app-logs/team-b
    cannotRead:
      - audit-logs
      - infra-logs

  - role: sre
    canRead:
      - app-logs
      - infra-logs
    cannotRead:
      - audit-logs

  - role: security
    canRead:
      - app-logs
      - audit-logs
      - infra-logs
    requiresMFA: true

  - role: audit-bot
    canRead:
      - audit-logs
    serviceAccount: true
    cannotWrite: true

  - role: break-glass
    canRead:
      - app-logs
      - audit-logs
      - infra-logs
    maxTTLHours: 24
    requiresApproval: true
    requiresIncidentTicket: true
Longer retention sounds safer but creates its own compliance risk: data you hold is data that can be subpoenaed, breached, or demanded in a GDPR right-to-erasure request.
Legal hold — same as audit logs

Implement retention via storage-native lifecycle policies, not application code. Application code can have bugs; storage lifecycle rules are enforced by the vendor. For AWS S3, use Object Lock with Governance or Compliance mode on the audit bucket, add a lifecycle rule to transition to Glacier after 1 year and DEEP_ARCHIVE after 7 years, and set an Expiration after 10 years.

Log access control and audit logging are the final layer in a defence-in-depth approach to Claude application observability. PII scrubbing keeps sensitive content out of logs; RBAC keeps logs out of the wrong hands; immutable audit stores keep the evidence of access tamper-proof; and automated policy-as-code keeps the whole system honest across team changes and audits.
Define narrow RBAC roles per log store and enforce them at the data-source layer, not just the UI.
Write audit logs to an immutable, separate store — never co-located with application logs.
Record every log query as an audit event, not just writes.
Implement break-glass as a first-class, time-limited, PR-gated procedure.
Export access metrics to Prometheus and alert on anomalies (audit write failure, query rate spike).
Enforce access policy as code in CI — policy drift is detected before it reaches production.
Set retention via storage lifecycle rules, not application logic, and review quarterly.
